summary: High‑octane second set with The Other One and classic jams
blurb: The show bursts with a thrashing The Other One and a seamless He's Gone → Smokestack Lightning → Comes A Time suite. Jerry’s guitar drives the energy while Brent’s keyboards and the twin drums create a psychedelic surge. Highlights include a Chinese‑dragon‑enhanced Space and a fiery rendition of The Mighty Quinn.
review: Feb 9, 1986 at the Henry J. Kaiser Convention Center captured one of the Dead’s most kinetic mid‑80s outings. The first set opened solidly with Jack Straw and a tight Deal, but it was the second set that truly ignited the night. A blistering rendition of The Mighty Quinn kicked things off before the band dove into Samson & Delilah and then launched into an unforgettable sequence: He's Gone segued straight into Smokestack Lightning, flowed through Comes A Time, and exploded into a monster drum groove that birthed a psychedelic Space. The dragon‑dance performance by the Chinese Symphony Orchestra added visual fireworks to the already soaring jam. From there, The Other One roared forward with relentless intensity, riding on Jerry’s classic tone (despite a minor fuzz on Samson) and Brent’s stellar, spacey keyboards, while Bob unleashed one of his fiercest Smokestack solos of the era. The twin‑drum attack from Mickey and Billy was described as “monstrous” and kept the momentum soaring through Around & Around into a joyous Good Lovin’. The encore’s gentle bow with It’s All Over Now Baby Blue gave fans a fitting, reflective close to an electrifying night.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are strongly positive, praising the high‑energy jams, iconic song sequences and standout performances; one recording is mixed but only notes patchy audio, not performance flaws. Consistent praise across multiple sources yields a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard source with lossless, crisp audio and no dropouts; reviewers highlight its clarity and full capture of the high‑octane performance.

key_highlights: Thrashing, high‑energy jam on The Other One
Classic He's Gone → Smokestack Lightning → Comes A Time sequence
Visually striking Chinese dragon during Space
Brent’s soaring keyboards and monster drum groove power the second set

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia was in top form, delivering powerful, classic tones that drove the intense jams on The Other One and The Mighty Quinn, with only a minor note of distortion on Samson.
band_performance.Bob: Weir unleashed a ferocious solo on Smokestack Lightning, delivering one of his best Brent‑era performances of the night.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s keyboards were stellar, providing spacey textures on Smokestack Lightning and soaring leads throughout the second‑set improvisations.
band_performance.Mickey: The twin‑drum attack was monstrous and psychedelic, laying down a thunderous groove that propelled Space and The Other One to new heights.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming teamed with Mickey for a high‑energy, monster‑groove foundation that kept the momentum relentless.
